title: organization — cache keys + tags

keys.description: The tenant's default Organization row (id + slug + name + logo_url). Read by every `me` payload + every server-side render. Warmed on TenantProvisioned + invalidated by DefaultOrganizationResolver on OrganizationDefaultChanged.
A single Organization row projection for tenant-plane API responses (excludes ops-only columns like `notes`).

Full tenant tree serialised as `[{ id, parent_id, tree_path, tree_depth, name, slug, status, is_default, sort_order }]`. Rebuilt on any parent_id change + any status/name/sort_order mutation. Used by the tenant sidebar picker + platform-admin tree renderer.

Every descendant id of the given Organization (for `is-ancestor-of?` / `is-descendant-of?` checks on the hot path). Backed by a LIKE-prefix query on tree_path + cached because per-request scoped-access decisions can hit this dozens of times.
Every ancestor id, in root-to-self order. Derived by splitting `tree_path` on the dot separator. Same TTL rationale as descendants.
Precomputed slot-utilisation payload for the entitlement bar UI ({ used, cap, pct }). Warmed by the entitlement observer + invalidated on any create / delete.
Slug → id lookup for `X-Organization-Id` header resolution when the header carries a slug instead of a ULID. Case-insensitive keying (slug is stored lowercase).

notes: All caches are tenant-namespaced — a `flushTag('tenant:{tenant_id}')` sweeps every Organization cache row for that tenant, which is the pattern used by `tenancy::TenantErased`.
The `organization-tree` tag is a super-tag over `tree` + `descendants` + `ancestors` so a single RebuildTreePathsJob completion can invalidate everything tree-shaped in one call.
TTLs are relatively long because Organizations are LOW-churn — a tenant may add one every few weeks. The tags make targeted invalidation fast so long TTLs don't produce stale reads.
